text: Visioner RT @NSTRIKE1231 : 🇺🇸 🇮🇷 What we know about the ceasefire between Iran and the U.S.: ▪️ The Iranian side claims the U.S. agreed to a 10-point plan under which Iran retains control over the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. forces withdraw from the region, and uranium enrichment continues. ▪️ Trump claims Iran accepted a 15-point U.S. plan, under which Iran will eliminate enriched uranium together with the U.S. and halt enrichment activities. ▪️ Negotiations are now expected to determine the final version of the agreement. 🔺 The gap between the positions of the U.S. and Iran remains significant, suggesting a final deal is unlikely — neither side is willing to concede on core demands, and whichever does would effectively be seen as losing. ▪️ For now, the situation amounts to a temporary pause in the war, reportedly lasting two weeks, during which both sides are likely preparing for further confrontation. See the latest updates with us: @NSTRIKE1231 tweet

Visioner ✈️ 🇫🇷 BIG | Formation of the First UAV Unit of the French Army The French Army has announced that its first UAV unit (a UAV company) has reached full operational readiness. The purpose of its formation is to expand reconnaissance and strike capabilities and to increase the effectiveness of enemy targeting through the integration of unmanned systems. 🔵 The unit combines reconnaissance UAVs, strike drones, and loitering munitions, forming a unified reconnaissance-strike network. This enables target detection, data transmission, and strike execution in real time. 🔵 The creation of this unit reflects a shift toward the systematic integration of UAVs into the structure of the land forces. This enhances combat capabilities, while also indicating a transformation in the nature of combined arms warfare and the growing role of unmanned systems. See the latest updates with us: @visionergeo tweet

↩️ Reply to OSINT Visioner 🇫🇷 🇵🇱 🇷🇴 NATO Unit Exercise in Romania On April 1, at the Cincu training range (Brașov County, Romania), French and Polish Army units, as part of a NATO multinational battalion tactical group, conducted live-fire training exercises using 🚛 main battle tanks Leclerc tank and 🚛 armored personnel carriers Rosomak. 🔷 The objective of the exercise was to improve unit coordination, enhance combat readiness, and ensure preparedness for conducting joint operations on NATO’s eastern flank. 🔵 During the training, elements of combined arms combat were practiced, involving tank, mechanized infantry, and reconnaissance units. Leclerc tank tanks carried out fire engagement tasks, Rosomak APCs supported troop movement and mechanized infantry operations, while armored vehicles Panhard VBL were used for reconnaissance and command functions. Special emphasis was placed on coordination, fire control, and the resilience of command and control systems. 🔵 The exercise demonstrates the formation of a combat-capable multinational force in Romania under French leadership. This increases NATO’s rapid response capabilities, while also contributing to heightened military activity along the eastern borders and rising regional tensions. tweet

Visioner 🇺🇦 🇪🇺 The Brave1 defense technology cluster announced the results of the first stage of the EU4UA Defence Tech program, within which 12 selected Ukrainian companies will receive grants of up to 150 thousand euros for the development of innovative air defense systems. The project, implemented jointly with the European Union, is aimed at creating asymmetric means to respond to modern threats: priority is given to interceptor drones and early detection systems for aerial targets, namely small radars. At the same time, as reported, the allocated funds cover the entire cycle of primary development - from the validation of engineering concepts to the creation of prototypes and their field tests. Also, the teams have exactly six months to bring their solutions to the stage of readiness for real combat conditions. tweet

"Again, on the uranium, we're watching it. We know what they have, and they will give it up. And we'll get it, and we'll…" ⚡️ 🇺🇸 🇮🇷 🇦🇪 🇸🇦 U.S. War Secretary Pete Hegseth said coordination with Gulf allies, including the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Qatar and Kuwait, will continue. On control of Iranian airspace, he pointed to the seven‑hour daytime and nighttime rescue operations as evidence, stating that Iran has no network capable of defending its skies, as it has been “dismantled, degraded, and destroyed.” Kurdistan 24 correspondent: "Mr. Kurd or Rahim Rashidi from Kurdistan. I am a representative of Kurdistan TV and Kurdistan 24 for today. I have two questions. Mr. Secretary, will your forces stay in the region and continue to defend and protect your allies and take responsibility for their security? The second question is, as you mentioned many times, that you have full control of Iranian airspace. Do you still have this control or not?" Secretary of War Pete Hegseth: "Well, thank you for the question. The chairman mentioned the UAE and Bahrain and Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, who have fought shoulder to shoulder with us, especially in defense of our facilities, of our people, and of their facilities and we appreciate that so that coordination, of course, will continue and then when it comes to the skies over Iran, I would just, again, I would point to Exhibit A and Exhibit B, seven hours in the day, seven hours at night. If any foreign adversary attempted to do the same over our soil, they would have no ability to do so. Will they have a system here or there Maybe. Do they have a network, a system that's capable of defending their skies? No, they don't. As the chairman laid out, that's been dismantled, degraded, and destroyed. So it's a slight distinction, but an important one." @wfwitness

Hegseth: The UAE, Bahrain, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and Kuwait — all of whom have fought shoulder to shoulder with us, especially in defense of our facilities and our people, as well as their own facilities. We appreciate that, and that coordination will, of course, continue.

🇺🇸 🇮🇱 NEW YORK TIMES | How Trump Took the U.S. to War with Iran On February 11, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu arrived at the White House Situation Room — a venue rarely used for in-person sessions with foreign leaders — and delivered an hour-long classified briefing to President Trump and his senior advisers. Netanyahu argued Iran was "ripe for regime change." His four-part plan promised Iran's missile program destroyed within weeks, the Strait of Hormuz kept open, minimal retaliation against U.S. interests, and Mossad-fomented domestic unrest to "finish the job." He also raised the possibility of Kurdish fighters entering Iran from Iraq. At one point, the Israeli team played a video montage of potential post-regime leaders — among them Reza Pahlavi, the exiled son of Iran's last shah. Trump's response was swift. "Sounds good to me," he told Netanyahu. Israeli officials took it as de facto approval. The next day, U.S. intelligence pushed back. CIA Director John Ratcliffe used a single word to describe the regime-change scenarios: "farcical." Secretary of State Marco Rubio translated: "In other words, it's bullshit." Rubio's own position was narrower: "If our goal is regime change or an uprising, we shouldn't do it. But if the goal is to destroy Iran's missile program, that's a goal we can achieve." When Trump turned to Gen. Dan Caine, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs, for his read on the Israeli pitch, Caine was candid: "Sir, this is, in my experience, standard operating procedure for the Israelis. They oversell, and their plans are not always well-developed. They know they need us, and that's why they're hard-selling." Caine flagged weapons depletion and Strait of Hormuz risks persistently — but stopped short of recommending against the operation. Trump absorbed all of it and moved past it. Regime change, he said, would be "their problem." Vice President J.D. Vance mounted the most sustained opposition, telling colleagues the war would be "a disaster." Vance — a Marine Corps veteran who had publicly opposed war with Iran before the 2024 election — warned of regional chaos, mass casualties, soaring gas prices, depleted U.S. munitions stockpiles, and a fracturing of Trump's political base. He argued: "A war against a regime with enormous will for survival could leave the United States in a far worse position to fight conflicts for some years." He had been in Azerbaijan during Netanyahu's Feb. 11 presentation and was not present for the initial pitch. He was for no strikes at all — but knowing Trump was likely to intervene, he tried to steer toward more limited action. When a large-scale campaign appeared inevitable, Vance argued it should at least be carried out with overwhelming force. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth was the most enthusiastic advocate. On February 26 — the day before Trump's final order — he told the group: "We're going to have to take care of the Iranians eventually, so we might as well do it now." In the final Situation Room meeting, Trump went around the table. Each adviser stated their position. The risks had been named. The divisions were clear. Then Trump said: "I think we need to do it." On February 27, 22 minutes before the operational deadline set by Caine, Trump issued the formal order: "Operation Epic Fury is approved. No aborts. Good luck." 🔴 @DDGeopolitics
